2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88bc0c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7042x3952+241+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2F5b%2Fdb56a885423c9c457bbc93c2fafb%2F2-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Tiffany Renee Johnson, Jabari Khaliq, Cedric Young, Kelvin Roston Jr., David Alan Anderson, E. Faye Butler, Matt DeCaro and Al’Jaleel McGhee in "Ma Rainey's Black Bottom."" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88bc0c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7042x3952+241+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2F5b%2Fdb56a885423c9c457bbc93c2fafb%2F2-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0707563/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7042x3952+241+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2F5b%2Fdb56a885423c9c457bbc93c2fafb%2F2-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88bc0c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7042x3952+241+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0d%2F5b%2Fdb56a885423c9c457bbc93c2fafb%2F2-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Goodman’s production is stoking fresh interest in Ma Rainey, a real blues artist based in Chicago in the 1920s.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In the play, Butler’s Rainey feels larger than her actual stature. Even standing in a large open space during our meeting, without amplification, Butler’s voice has a melodic <i>boom</i>. In a way, she speaks like the singer.</p><p>“I think the diva is the cover,” Butler says of her character. “She knows she's talented. She’s always been a performer, and she’s used it. Performance has gotten her through life.”</p><p>To embody Ma Rainey on stage, Butler dove into the essence of the character.</p><p>“She's a very sexual presence, too,” said Butler. “She's aware of her sexuality. And in that time, we're talking about the 1920s, that was something big to do, to be a Black woman who was openly available to their sexuality and not afraid of that.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/859415a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9736c3d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/650f9b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Actress E. Faye Butler holds a costume in her dressing room at the Goodman Theater downtown, Tuesday, April 21, 2026. Butler is starring in the titular role of the theater’s production of August Wilson’s “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom”." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/650f9b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a93b4cd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/650f9b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc1%2F53%2Fc202a7ae43109725f78fb7652bf0%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-022.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Actress E. Faye Butler holds a costume in her dressing room. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In the opening scene of the play, Ma Rainey arrives fashionably, clad in fur after a mixup regarding her luxurious automobile. In real life, Komara says Rainey was also known to travel in style.</p><p>“Many blues singers [back then] traveled by train,” he explained. “She had one of the early buses. That says a lot about where she was as a performer that she could tour in style on a bus. She didn't have to rely on railroad timetables or anything like that. She could bring her and her whole band under one roof in this bus.”</p><p>Back at the Goodman, Butler marvels at how a Black woman, born in the mid 1880s, was able to become such a commanding presence. Underneath the big voice and flashy wardrobes, Ma Rainey was a complicated character. Born in rural Georgia, the singer spent a great deal of time in Chicago, where she recorded music for Paramount Records.</p><p>“She was a dark-skinned, African American woman,” said Butler. “She was larger than most people would think, and those things went against her in that period. She didn't have ‘good hair.’ All those things fight against you. She was always fighting to get to the top, and once she got there, I think she said, ‘No one is ever going to pull me down again.’”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4e0000" name="image-4e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/735bb27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3e0d1f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47efa86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Actress E. Faye Butler sits in her dressing room at the Goodman Theater downtown, Tuesday, April 21, 2026. Butler is starring in the titular role of the theater’s production of August Wilson’s “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom”. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47efa86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3d03f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47efa86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F96%2F4f%2F0653a79441ae957c011d12861edb%2Fma-raineys-260423-mad-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Butler performs Ma Rainey’s “Black Bottom” dance onstage in the role. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As Ma Rainey, Butler's power is on full display during the onstage rendition of one of the blues singer’s most popular tunes: the titular “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom.”</p><p>“It is a very real dance,” said Butler. “It was a slap on both of your legs, touching your buttocks, which was really provocative in the 20s, to slap your butt, and then to lick your finger, and slap your butt — ooh, that in itself, was not to be done.”</p><p>The provocative dance was not the only way the savvy singer went against the grain. Komara said many artists in that time period went broke during the Great Depression when banks failed. But Ma Rainey had invested in real estate. Komara said she also owned a movie theater in Columbus, Ga.. After she retired from performing on the road, she lived there, in her own building, until her death in 1939.</p><p>“She was a woman in a time when men were in power and they were telling her what to do,” said Butler, “but she wasn't going to have anyone telling her what to do. So she took charge of her own career.”</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Bears NFL draft primer: when it starts, who they may pick and how to watch
entries.summary: <p>Everything you need to know about the NFL draft, which starts Thursday, and the Bears’ role in it:</p><h3><b>When is the draft?</b></h3><p>Round 1 starts at 7 p.m. Thursday. Rounds 2 and 3 are Friday night, with the first pick coming at 6. Rounds 4-7 begin at 11 a.m. Saturday.</p><h3><b>Where is the draft?</b></h3><p>In Pittsburgh, outside the Steelers’ Stadium. What started conference rooms has expanded to fan events across the country. The NFL moved the draft out of its New York home in 2015, starting a two-year stay at the Auditorium Theatre. Since leaving Chicago, the draft has been held in Philadelphia, Dallas, Nashville, Cleveland, Las Vegas, Kansas City, Detroit and Green Bay. The 2020 draft was held virtually because of the coronavirus.</p><h3><b>Where will be the Bears be?</b></h3><p>All NFL teams keep their decision-makers at home for the draft. The Bears’ draft room, on the second floor of Halas Hall, will be run by general manager Ryan Poles and coach Ben Johnson, with assistant GM Jeff King, front office personnel, scouts and assistant coaches contributing when needed.</p><p>The team is throwing a draft party for fans from 6-10 p.m. at Soldier Field, with current and former players expected to attend.</p><h3><b>Where can I watch the draft?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>On ABC-7, ESPN and NFL Network.</p><h3><b>When do the Bears pick?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>They hold the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/22/nfl-draft-bears-25-overall-selection0-history-shows-stars-hard-to-find-tyler-linderbaum-santonio-holmes-ted-washington" target="_blank" >25<sup>th</sup> overall pick</a> in Round 1; No. 57 and 60 in Round 2; No. 89 in Round 3; No. 129 in Round 4; and No. 239 and No. 241 in Round 7. The Bears acquired Pick 60 from the Bills in the DJ Moore trade, Pick 129 from the Rams on a draft-day trade last year and Pick 239 from the Browns in last season’s Joe Tryon-Shoyinka deal.</p><p>Poles is notorious for trading back, particularly on Days 2 and 3. Don’t be surprised if the Bears find a way to land a pick — or two — between No. 129 and 239 by moving back in Rounds 2, 3 or 4.</p><h3><b>Let me rephrase: what time do they pick?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Last year’s No. 25 overall pick was made just before 10 p.m. This year, there are only eight minutes between selections, rather than 10. So maybe around 9:15 Thursday? It’s an inexact science.</p><h3><b>That seems late.</b></h3><h3></h3><p>The Bears haven’t drafted this deep into Round 1 since 2011, when they took Gabe Carimi 29<sup>th</sup>. They didn’t have first-round picks in 2019 or 2020 after trading for edge rusher Khalil Mack, or in 2022 after trading up to draft quarterback Justin Fields.</p><h3><b>Weren’t they supposed to have an extra Round 3 pick?</b></h3><p>When the Bears lost then-assistant GM Ian Cunningham to the Falcons’ GM job, they argued they were entitled to a compensatory third-round pick in 2026 and another in 2027 as part of an NFL program that rewards teams who develop — and then lose — minority head coach and GM candidates. The NFL ruled, though, that the Falcons’ lead decision-maker was president of football Matt Ryan and not Cunningham, and denied the Bears the compensatory picks. The Bears appealed in person, flying to the NFL’s offices in New York City, but to no avail. That selection would have been the last pick of Round 3 in this year’s draft.</p><h3><b>Who will the Bears pick Thursday night?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>The Bears’ first-round pick figures to be a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/21/bears-nfl-draft-defense-general-manager-ryan-poles-once-did-offense-edge-rusher-defensive-tackle-safety-thursday-round-1-pittsburgh" target="_blank" >defensive lineman, offensive tackle or safety. </a>Most experts believe that the Bears should have their choice of a few different starting edge rushers if they keep their pick at No. 25. Read the Sun-Times' final mock draft <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/21/mock-draft-will-the-bears-take-blake-miller-kayden-mcdonald-or-t-j-parker-at-no-25" target="_blank" >here</a>.</p><h3><b>What about the (dare I say) Packers?</b></h3><p>They don’t have a first-round pick. They traded their selection, No. 20, to the Cowboys in the Micah Parsons deal on the eve of last season. The division rival Lions pick 17<sup>th</sup> and the Vikings 18<sup>th</sup>.</p><h3><b>Who’s going first overall?</b></h3><p>Indiana quarterback Fernando Mendoza will be the Raiders’ first choice. The last Hoosier picked first overall was Corbett Davis in 1937.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Halfway to the Hall? Cubs' Craig Counsell's mini-milestone is reason to wonder
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a>’ <b>Craig Counsell</b> clocked in for work Thursday needing three more wins to hit the not-so-major milestone of 900 for his managerial career.</p><p>There probably won’t be a parade to mark the occasion for the skipper ranked No. 78 on baseball’s all-time list.</p><p>But Counsell is only 55 and, by the looks and sounds of him, has many years of “Ws” left in the tank.</p><p>“You want a high-quality life and to live life to the fullest, but if you’re doing something you love to do, you keep doing it,” he told the Sun-Times.</p><p>Let’s say Counsell were to keep managing without interruption until age 65, while maintaining his career winning percentage of .534. By then, he’d be in — or, at worst, right on the doorstep of — the top 20. And that’s Hall of Fame territory, especially, in Counsell’s case, if he were to add a World Series championship along the way to the two he won as a player.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/22/wrigley-rat-spooks-phillies-amuses-cubs-fans" >Wrigley rat spooks Phillies, annoys Cubs brass</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/22/cubs-win-eighth-straight-behind-matthew-boyds-return-ben-browns-relief-work" target="_blank" >Cubs win eighth straight behind Matthew Boyd’s return, Ben Brown’s relief work</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Zooming past <b>Al Lopez</b>, <b>Earl Weaver</b>, <b>Tommy Lasorda </b>and more than a dozen others whose plaques reside in Cooperstown, N.Y. is kind of fun to think about, isn’t it?</p><p>“I don’t know if I’ve ever thought about it in those terms,” Counsell said. “The thing it really makes me think of is when you’re in a game and [the Reds’] <b>Terry Francona</b>’s on the other side or [recently retired] <b>Bruce Bochy</b>’s on the other side. No matter who you are, you have this respect for the longevity that they’ve achieved. I respect their longevity more than anything.”</p><p>Francona, 11th on the all-time wins list with 2,049 entering Thursday’s games, is one of four active managers ahead of Counsell, the others being the Tigers’ <b>A.J. Hinch </b>(977), the Dodgers’ <b>Dave Roberts </b>(960) and the Rays’ <b>Kevin Cash </b>(909).</p><p>Counsell is under contract with the Cubs through 2028.</p><p>“We all think about our futures and our lives, of course, like, ‘How do I want to spend life?’ ” he said. “But I don’t have [a timeline] in mind. I’ve learned that these seasons, you take what’s in front of you and try to do the best you can at it and enjoy it and work hard at it, and then you move forward. I think long-term planning like that is useless, honestly, because life changes too fast, especially as you get older.”</p><p>Does that mean we shouldn’t start inquiring about an eventual contract extension with the North Siders?</p><p>“Man, that is so far away from me right now,” he said. “Definitely too soon.”</p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Trump reclassifies state-licensed medical marijuana as a less-dangerous drug in a historic shift
entries.summary: <p>President Donald Trump’s acting attorney general on Thursday signed an order <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-reclassify-marijuana-cannibas-legal-marijuana-federal-ab2aec5865dd140bac00b7cef5de89c5" target="_blank" >reclassifying state-licensed medical marijuana</a> as a less-dangerous drug, a major policy shift long sought by advocates who said cannabis should never have been treated like heroin by the federal government.</p><p>The order signed by Todd Blanche does not legalize marijuana for medical or recreational use under federal law. But it does change the way it’s regulated, shifting licensed medical marijuana from Schedule I — reserved for drugs without medical use and with high potential for abuse — to the less strictly regulated Schedule III. It also gives licensed medical marijuana operators a major tax break and eases some barriers to researching cannabis.</p><p>The Trump administration also said it was jump-starting the process for reclassifying marijuana more broadly, setting a hearing to begin in late June.</p><p>Trump told his administration in December to work as quickly as possible to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/trump-marijuana-executive-order-bc1e3e5376105fdc6240982b10f74f6f" target="_blank" >reclassify marijuana</a>. On Saturday, as the Republican president signed <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/ibogaine-psychedelic-trump-fda-ptsd-veterans-kennedy-a9940fa57fa1457fc064eb5165003524" target="_blank" >an unrelated executive order</a> about psychedelics, he seemed to express frustration that it was taking so long.</p><p>Blanche said Thursday that the Department of Justice was “delivering on President Trump’s promise” to expand Americans’ access to medical treatment options. “This rescheduling action allows for research on the safety and efficacy of this substance, ultimately providing patients with better care and doctors with more reliable information,” he said in a statement.</p><h2>What the marijuana reclassification order does</h2><p>Blanche’s action Iargely legitimizes medical marijuana programs in the 40 states that have adopted them. It sets up an expedited system for state-licensed medical marijuana producers and distributors to register with the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration.</p><p>It makes clear that cannabis researchers won’t be penalized for obtaining state-licensed marijuana or marijuana-derived products for use in their work, and it grants state-licensed medical marijuana companies a windfall by allowing them, for the first time, to deduct business expenses on their federal taxes.</p><p>Any marijuana-derived medicine approved by the Food and Drug Administration is similarly listed in Schedule III, it said.</p><p>The order represents a major policy shift for the U.S. government, which has continued its longstanding marijuana prohibition — dating to <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/general-news-62f4692d778b462db36af7c8ca120e06" target="_blank" >the Marihuana Tax Act of 1937</a> — even as nearly all the states have approved cannabis use in some form. Two dozen states plus Washington, D.C., have authorized adult recreational use of marijuana, 40 have medical marijuana systems, and eight others allow low-THC cannabis or CBD oil for medical use. Only Idaho and Kansas ban marijuana outright.</p><p>The regulation of medical marijuana has come a long way since California became the first state to adopt it in 1996, Blanche wrote.</p><p>“Today the vast majority of States maintain comprehensive licensing frameworks governing cultivation, processing, distribution, and dispensing of marijuana for medical purposes,” Blanche wrote. “Taken as a whole, they demonstrate a sustained capacity to achieve the public-interest objectives ... including protecting public health and safety and preventing the diversion of controlled substances into illicit channels.”</p><p>The president of the American Trade Association for Cannabis and Hemp, Michael Bronstein, called it “the most significant federal advancement in cannabis policy in over 50 years.”</p><p>“This action recognizes what Americans have long known, cannabis is medicine,” he said in a written statement.</p><p><b>Critic calls the order ‘a tax break to Big Weed’</b></p><p>The Trump administration’s decision drew derision from marijuana legalization opponent Kevin Sabet, the chief executive of Smart Approaches to Marijuana. Sabet said that while marijuana research is necessary, “there are many ways to increase our knowledge without giving a tax break to Big Weed and sending a confusing message about marijuana’s harms to the American public.”</p><p>“With this move, we are now confronted with the most pro-drug administration in our history,” Sabet said in a text message. “Policy is now being dictated by marijuana CEOs, psychedelics investors, and podcasters in active addiction.”</p><p>Marijuana or marijuana-derived products that are not distributed through a state medical marijuana program will continue to be classified in Schedule I.</p><p>Schedule III drugs are defined as having moderate to low potential for physical and psychological dependence. Some critics of the industry have suggested that legalization in the states has led to stronger and stronger cannabis products, which need to be researched rather than categorized less strictly than before.</p><p><b>The efforts to reclassify marijuana</b></p><p>The Justice Department under President Joe Biden, a Democrat, had <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/marijuana-rescheduling-drug-policy-biden-15b43441670757b0c2bfa36731e47d07" target="_blank" >proposed to reclassify marijuana</a>, eliciting nearly 43,000 formal public comments. The DEA was still in the review process when Trump succeeded Biden, and Trump ordered that process to move along as quickly as legally possible.</p><p>Blanche’s order sidestepped the review process by relying on a provision of federal law that allows the attorney general to determine the appropriate classification for drugs that the U.S. must regulate pursuant to an international treaty.</p><p>It was unclear how the order might affect operations in states where licensed recreational marijuana shops also sell to medical patients. In Washington state, which in 2012 became one of the first states to legalize the adult use of marijuana, 302 of 460 licensed stores have endorsements allowing them to sell tax-free cannabis products to registered patients.</p><p>Many Republicans oppose loosening marijuana restrictions. More than 20 Republican senators, several of them staunch Trump allies, signed a letter last year urging the president to keep the current standards.</p><p>Trump has made his crusade against other drugs, especially fentanyl, a feature of his second term, <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/boat-strikes-us-military-drugs-pacific-108d10bbd3d19c34b8959602222e22e6" target="_blank" >ordering U.S. military attacks</a> on Venezuelan and other boats the administration insists are ferrying drugs. He signed another executive order declaring fentanyl a weapon of mass destruction.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Boy, 16, found fatally shot in abandoned, stolen car in South Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Detectives continue to look for witnesses and any video evidence after a 16-year-old boy was found shot in the head in the backseat of a stolen car riddled with bullets in the South Chicago neighborhood Tuesday night. </p><p>A security officer flagged down a police officer to tell them about an apparently abandoned Hyundai Elantra, which was reported stolen and was still running in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/21/man-fatally-shot-in-south-chicago-car" target="_blank" >8600 block of South DuSable Lake Shore Drive about 8:30 p.m.,</a> according to police report. </p><p>Inside officers found the unidentified boy in the back seat, shot in the head, according to the report, which said the car had "multiple bullet strikes."</p><p>The boy was pronounced dead on the scene by paramedics and was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center. </p><p>Officers learned there was a call of shots fired and criminal damage to property in the 8600 block of South Drexel Avenue. When police got there, they found several vehicles hit by gunfire and a "large amount of blood in the street," the report said.</p><p>No arrests have been made. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

>Bears’ exit from Chicago is closer to reality as House passes bill helping Arlington Heights bid</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-h/" target="_blank" >Tina Sfondeles</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-k/" target="_blank" >Mawa Iqbal</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-u/" target="_blank" >Matt Trunfio</a></h5><h5></h5><p><b>Arlington Heights Bears?</b>: In an attempt to keep the Chicago Bears from leaving for Indiana, Illinois lawmakers advanced a measure Wednesday that would give the team property tax certainty while also providing some property tax relief for homeowners.</p><p><b>The measure</b>: The House voted 78-32 to OK the megaproject proposal, which still needs approval from the Illinois Senate. It would incentivize the Bears to build a domed stadium in Arlington Heights by negotiating payments in lieu of property taxes based on assessments. It also directs those funds toward property tax relief for homeowners — near the megaproject and throughout Illinois.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The bill represents the first major legislative step toward ensuring the Bears stay in Illinois, after a monthslong tug of war between Illinois and the Hoosier state. The thorny details of infrastructure funding for Arlington Heights must still be dealt with before lawmakers' spring session adjourns next month.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>THE WATCHDOGS 👀</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-660000" name="image-660000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fd8e901/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F27%2F87b7358e44b8b2c8e2fac262bd38%2Fbankfailure-260201-3.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/affd8dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F27%2F87b7358e44b8b2c8e2fac262bd38%2Fbankfailure-260201-3.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dc0d191/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa5%2F27%2F87b7358e44b8b2c8e2fac262bd38%2Fbankfailure-260201-3.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Metropolitan Capital Bank and Trust, which declared bank failure, located at 9 E. block traffic as contract talks with CPS stall</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-c/" target="_blank" >Sarah Karp</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-q/" target="_blank" >Emmanuel Camarillo</a></h5><p><b>Union action</b>: Lunchroom workers represented by the union Unite Here Local 1 have been working to negotiate a contract with Chicago Public Schools for more than 11 months. To "sound the alarm" on what they see as unfair and unequal wages, they plan to protest Thursday. Several workers say they'll sit and block traffic downtown as an act of civil disobedience.</p><p><b>At issue</b>: The two biggest sticking points, according to Unite Here, are wages and staffing levels. Lunchroom workers including cooks, porters who clean kitchens and attendants who serve food are among the lowest-paid full-time workers in the school district. Their average salaries range from about $23,400 to $37,400, according to a recent CPS employee roster.</p><p><b>Zooming in</b>: Kimberly Penson, 61, is the sole cook at Greene Elementary School in Bridgeport. She has worked for CPS for 21 years and makes an annual salary of about $34,000. Penson says she feels deeply disrespected by the district’s leadership. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-f/" target="_blank" ><b>READ class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Indie rockers Ratboys evolve from ‘country mouse’ to ‘city rat’ on latest album</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-ju/" target="_blank" >Selena Fragassi</a></h5><p>In other rat-related coverage, one of the most solid bands in the city's indie music scene, Ratboys, are in the midst of a banner run.</p><p>The quartet’s sixth record, "Singin’ to an Empty Chair," released Feb. 6, comprises 11 tracks drenched in twangy folk, pop-rock and distortion, and all beating with Midwestern heart. </p><p>It’s a similar vibe to 2023’s "The Window," which helped put the band on the map and led to their first headlining tour across the country; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-tl/" target="_blank" >a Lollapalooza debut</a> a couple years later, not long after singer/guitarist Julia Steiner worked on the festival’s artist transport team; and an appearance on "CBS Saturday Sessions."</p><p>Ten years after forming at the University of Notre Dame, the band is going strong, having just wrapped a national tour and gearing up to play in Europe starting next month.</p><p>But Chicago is still home for Steiner, bassist Sean Neumann and drummer Marcus Nuccio.</p><p>"In Chicago, we all lift each other up. It feels very inclusive … and it’s been that way for a long time," Nuccio said of the city's music scene.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-tr/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiujdhl-l-tj/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: NFL Network exec Charlie Yook set for first NFL Draft with ESPN — 30 years late
entries.summary: <p>There was a time when Charlie Yook, the longtime production head at NFL Network, dreamed of working for ESPN.</p><p>In the mid-1990s at Miami University, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/4/29/22409763/glenview-charlie-yook-nfl-network-draft-glenbrook-south-rich-eisen-daniel-jeremiah-cleveland" target="_blank" >the Glenbrook South grad</a> and his friends religiously watched “SportsCenter,” “The Big Show” version with Dan Patrick and Keith Olbermann. Then they’d stay up and watch the late “SportsCenter.” A journalism major, Yook sent notes to a couple ESPN officials and never heard back.</p><p>“It was definitely the dream spot coming out of college, especially during that time when ‘SportsCenter’ was all we were watching,” said Yook, who graduated in 1996. “But life takes turns here and there.”</p><p>That it does, and 30 years later, when ESPN bought NFL Network in a deal that took effect April 1, Yook became an ESPN employee. The networks’ first joint production is taking place this week in Pittsburgh, where they’ll produce separate broadcasts of the NFL Draft. Yook, now the vice president and executive producer for content production, will continue to oversee NFL Network. He’ll report to ESPN executive vice president for sports production Mike McQuade.</p><p>The draft will be a crossover event that will include NFL Network draft analyst Daniel Jeremiah appearing on ESPN and “Good Morning Football” co-host Kyle Brandt, a Stevenson grad, reuniting with “GMF” co-host turned ESPN analyst Peter Schrager.</p><p>“The shares are more traditional NFL Network going over on the ESPN side, but we’re truly a joint family moving forward, so we’ll figure out everything else afterward,” Yook, 52, said Wednesday before a 12-hour day in the production truck.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Charlie Yook graduated from Miami University in 1996 hoping to work at ESPN. He finally has that chance.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>NFL Network</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>From that truck, Yook, who has led NFL Network’s draft coverage since 2014, will have more toys at his disposal than he has ever had, including three drones and augmented reality graphics.</p><p>“We flew the drone yesterday during rehearsal, and the pilot was incredible,” Yook said. “It was like up, down, below the bridge, through traffic. I’m like, ‘Guys, we should just use a shot going to break every time.’ It was awesome.”</p><p>Jeremiah’s analysis will be the focus of NFL Network’s coverage. He has become a leader in draft analysis, and Yook was effusive in his praise of Jeremiah’s player-breakdown tapes.</p><p>“You can only say so much in 35-40 seconds, but the tease I would give you is just watch how he goes through these tapes,” Yook said. “You’re gonna learn something about every player. The guy’s an animal, and I mean that in the best way.”</p><p>As excited as Yook is to be at “The Worldwide Leader in Sports,” it wasn’t easy for him or anyone at NFL Network to hear all the talk about its future while the merger made its way through government regulators. The deal also included ESPN absorbing the NFL’s fantasy football operation and distribution rights to the NFL Red Zone channel, and the league took a 10% equity stake in ESPN.</p><p>“I mean, it’s hard,” Yook said. “For me, it was 20 seasons at the NFL and hard just because you’ve been somewhere for two decades. Change is never easy. But there’s opportunity here. They’re called ‘The Worldwide Leader’ for a reason. Their scope is beyond football. And, oh, by the way, they have the Super Bowl across the street from our office [at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, California] on Valentine’s Day of 2027. We’re gonna be busy.”</p><p>Long before the merger, NFL Network began cutting back on programming and talent. Like with many such sales, it had to be leaned out before being sold. But Yook is hopeful that the network will be able to expand its programming under ESPN’s leadership. In particular, Yook is looking at the seven games NFL Network will carry next season.</p><p>“If we’re going to work with a partner — we are ESPN, it has to be the vernacular — how would you like to do game coverage better? We didn’t travel for our games [last year]. Would it be that difficult to bring a smaller set, two to three talent or an existing ESPN show to do pre, half and post for these games? I don’t know. The games are going to be the most important thing for us.”</p><p>But Yook wanted to assure NFL Network viewers that it will remain NFL Network.</p><p>“It’s not becoming ESPN3; it’s not going to air on a different channel,” he said. “It’s like SEC Network is its own network, but ESPN owns it. It’s still NFL Network. It’s just owned by ESPN.”</p><h3>Remote patrol</h3><p>There’s plenty of Bears pre-draft coverage on TV tonight, including Chicago Sports Network’s “Big Pro Football Show” at 6, Fox 32’s “Bears Draft Live” also at 6 and NBC 5’s “Countdown to the Draft” at 6:30.</p><p>• Cubs radio voice <b>Pat Hughes</b> is among the 24 nominees for the 2026 Radio Hall of Fame class. Inductees will be announced May 20 and honored Oct. 8 in Chicago.</p><p>• Former ESPN 1000 executive <b>Randy Merkin</b> soon will release his second book, “Beyond the Glass: More stories from a sports radio producer,” from Eckhartz Press.</p><p>• CHSN will air a “Kids Day” broadcast of the Nationals-White Sox game Sunday that will include a young fan announcing the Sox’ lineup and kid-focused graphics.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Hombre muerto, 3 mujeres heridas en tiroteo en La Villita
entries.summary: <p>Un hombre fue asesinado y tres mujeres resultaron heridas en un tiroteo en el vecindario de La Villita el miércoles por la tarde, según informó la policía.</p><p>El incidente ocurrió a eso de las 4:30 p.m. cuando un hombre y tres mujeres se encontraban cerca de la acera en la cuadra 3400 al oeste de la calle 26th. Un tirador desconocido abrió fuego, alcanzando a los cuatro, dijo la policía.</p><p>El hombre, de 46 años, recibió varios disparos y fue trasladado al Hospital Mount Sinai, donde fue declarado muerto. Su nombre no ha sido divulgado.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e20000" name="module-e20000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>De las tres mujeres:</p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-a9583040-3f3d-11f1-9693-7f7ab4ac18fa"><li>A una de ellas, de edad desconocida, le dispararon en la parte baja de la espalda y llegó por sus propios medios al Hospital Mount Sinai, donde fue reportada en estado grave.</li><li>La segunda, de 25 años, recibió un disparo en la cara y fue trasladada al Mount Sinai, donde fue reportada en condición estable.</li><li>La tercera, de 47 años, fue herida en el brazo y trasladada al Mount Sinai, donde fue reportada en condición estable.</li></ul><p>Los detectives del Área 4 están investigando.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Woman accused of shooting 3 men outside Irving Park neighborhood tavern
entries.summary: <p>A 27-year-old Humboldt Park woman is due in court Thursday accused of shooting three men who were denied entry to an Irving Park neighborhood tavern earlier this month, police said. </p><p>Isathtia E. Perez is charged with attempted murder, aggravated battery, aggravated possession of a loaded weapon and aggravated discharge of a gun, police said.</p><p>A motive was not given by police. </p><p>On April 12 about 2:45 a.m., <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/12/irving-park-shooting" target="_blank" >Perez allegedly opened fire</a> from down the block at the three victims: 37, 31 and 38, who were outside near Irving Park Road and Francisco Avenue after being being denied entry to a nearby bar, according to a police report. </p><p>Court records show Perez, a delivery driver for a cookie shop, fired 14 times. </p><p>The 37-year-old man suffered multiple gunshot wounds and was taken in critical condition to Advocate Illinois Masonic Medical Center, police said at the time. The other two victims were in fair condition, and were taken to Saint Francis Hospital in Evanston and Advocate Illinois Medical Center. </p><p>Perez, of the Humboldt Park neighborhood, was due in court Thursday. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Mother of Chicago cop killed by partner says 8-year sentence is 'extremely severe' for man police were chasing
entries.summary: <p>The mother of a Chicago police officer who was fatally shot by her partner told a judge Thursday that a man pleading guilty to charges related to the death “deserves a sentence that reflects the truth of their role — not the burden of institutional failures beyond his control.”</p><p>The comments by officer Krystal Rivera’s mother, Yolanda, came as Jaylin Arnold pleaded guilty to gun and drug charges during a packed hearing in a Bridgeview courtroom. Arnold, 28, accepted a deal that comes with an eight-year prison sentence, though his attorney said he expects Arnold will likely serve closer to three years.</p><p>Still, Yolanda Rivera said the sentence was “extremely severe” and called for her daughter’s former partner, Carlos Baker, to be held accountable.</p><p>“Krystal deserves justice,” said Yolanda Rivera, who has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >filed a wrongful death lawsuit</a> alleging that Baker had been struggling to accept her daughter’s decision to end their romantic relationship when he fatally shot her.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >The shooting</a> happened as Baker and Rivera chased Arnold into a Chatham apartment on June 5, 2025.</p><p>After Baker kicked down the door of the apartment, the officers encountered a second man, Adrian Rucker, pointing a rifle in their direction, according to police and court records.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/17/chicago-police-krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-shooting-body-camera-copa" target="_blank" >Body camera footage released last week</a> shows that Baker turned and appeared to stumble after kicking in the door and seeing the gun. He then fired a single shot that struck Krystal Rivera.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-200000" name="image-200000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59a6671/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/be6c73e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Police Officer Carlos Baker" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7bf2ec5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/212dcef/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2400x1347+0+198/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2b%2F32%2F3a0bd2ee4093b469f6246be0b191%2F14df0e91-00ba-4b92-86fc-affa090b7b9f.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago police officer Carlos Baker</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago Police Department</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Baker waited more than 90 seconds before checking on Krystal Rivera as she was gasping for breath. Baker allegedly “left her to die,” according to Yolanda Rivera's lawsuit.</p><p>“The officer failed to render aid when it was critically needed,” Yolanda Rivera said in court. “It’s difficult to accept Arnold should bear the weight.”</p><p>When officers searched the apartment, they found a second rifle, a Glock handgun and ammunition, according to Cook County prosecutors. They also found bags and jars of heroin, cocaine and marijuana, as well as fake IDs from multiple states that had Rucker’s photo.</p><p>Arnold was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/21/chicago-crime-shooting-police-officer-krystal-rivera-cpd" target="_blank" >picked up on a warrant</a> for a parole violation weeks later, an arrest report shows. He was found with 11 bags of suspected crack cocaine.</p><p>Both Rucker and Arnold had long criminal records, according to court documents. Arnold was convicted on gun charges in March 2023 and sentenced to seven years in prison. He has been sentenced to prison in at least two other gun cases.</p><p>Arnold pleaded guilty to a felony gun possession charge Thursday in exchange for an eight-year sentence. It will run concurrently with a three-year sentence for a separate drug possession charge. He has already received almost a year credit for time served.</p><p>“I think my client got a just sentence based on what he was charged with,” said Arnold’s attorney, Michael Clancy. “I think everyone has to remember he was not charged with the death of the officer for having anything to do with causing the death of the officer.”</p><p></p><p>Baker's attorney declined to comment Thursday.</p><p></p><p>Baker has been the subject of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/06/27/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-gresham-district-police-discipline-copa-civilian-office-police-accountability" target="_blank" >more than a dozen misconduct complaints</a>. It’s an unusually high amount as only 5% of Chicago police had six or more misconduct complaints from 2018 through 2023, according to data collected by the Invisible Institute.</p><p></p><p>He picked up five complaints as a probationary officer, when he could have been summarily fired because he had few union protections. </p><p>In one case, he was accused of flashing a gun at a woman he’d met online while she was on a date with another man at a North Side bar, records show. The woman later refused to cooperate with investigators, and Baker faced no disciplinary action in that case.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2025/06/05/chicago-police-officer-shot-cpd-crime-shooting" target="_blank" >Chicago police officer was ‘unintentionally’ shot and killed by partner during Chatham foot pursuit</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/17/chicago-police-krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-shooting-body-camera-copa" target="_blank" >Bodycam video shows Chicago cop took cover for 90 seconds after fatally shooting his partner Krystal Rivera</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/12/10/krystal-rivera-carlos-baker-chicago-police-department-wrongful-death-lawsuit" target="_blank" >Family of Chicago cop killed in botched chase sues Chicago Police Department and partner who shot her</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: We love trees. So why isn't Arbor Day a bigger deal?
entries.summary: <p>Happy Arbor Day! Did it sneak up on you, again? Or are you ready with the... well, not a lot to do on Arbor Day. No gifts to give, no cards to send. No parties to throw unless you're a municipality, and even then, they celebrate by doing the same thing they do all year long: put a few trees in the ground. It's like treating your wife to dinner at home and a TV show for her birthday.</p><p>It doesn't make sense. Love is elusive, fleeting, heartbreaking, yet Valentine's Day is huge. Trees are everywhere, permanent, uplifting. Yet we give them the cold shoulder. Why isn't Arbor Day a bigger deal?</p><p>"That's a really good question," said David Horvath, a certified arborist with The Davey Tree Expert Company. "It doesn't get much mention in the media. You guys aren't reporting on it."</p><p>Oh right. Our fault. Maybe so. This is my first Arbor Day column in 30 years. Horvath must have detected my air of injury, because he mused that lack of attention might be a good thing.</p><p>"We're doing a pretty good job, preserving trees," he said. "We don't have a lot of news stories about hundreds of acres being clear cut."</p><p>Not yet. That may be coming, with the Trump administration dismantling the U.S. Forest Service and going gaga for logging.</p><p>It's a good time to reaffirm our love of trees. Trees are cool, and very Chicago. How so? For starters, we have a direct, familial link to Arbor Day: J. Sterling Morton, who created Arbor Day in 1872 as a way to forest treeless Nebraska. Fifty years later, his son Joy Morton, founder of Morton Salt, created the Morton Arboretum on his country estate in west suburban Lisle. </p><p>Arbor Day was a state-by-state affair until 1970, when Richard Nixon established National Arbor Day as the last Friday in April (though states still celebrate at peak planting times. Texas Arbor Day is the first Friday in November).</p><p>The city of Chicago has an estimated 3.5 million trees, and I wish I could tell you a dozen tree stories. Space limits us to one. In 1972, students voted for an Illinois state tree. The white oak won. At Austin High School, however, students disagreed, pooled their money — each chipped in a penny — and bought a black oak, which they planted in the school courtyard. "The black student body felt a closer identification with this type of oak," the Chicago Daily News helpfully explained. (The tree, alas, is no longer there, according to the Chicago Public Schools. "No sign of the black oak tree," said Ben Pagani, of CPS, who added engineers were sent to scope out the situation).</p><p>"Trees are really important to our humanity, they're part of our civil identity, our health," said Horvath. "So many memories of trees, with family members. A very important fixture in our lives and memories."</p><p>So how to celebrate? Check online for events near you. Apple cider seems appropriate. If you've got space, grab a free sapling being handed out and give it a home. If you don't have a yard, a pot will suffice for a time — for several years I had a gorgeous Chicago hardy fig growing in our dining room, until my wife banished it to the yard. Careful mulching and burlap screening got it through its first winter; I wasn't quite so thorough for the second, and am still waiting to see if it's alive.</p><p>Or figure out what your favorite tree is. Horvath plumped for sugar maple — good choice. I flew the flag for ginkgo biloba — I planted one in my front yard. A gorgeous tree, glossy, fan-shaped leaves, a favorite of Frank Lloyd Wright. Male of course — you didn't know, did ya, that some trees have males, producing pollen, and females, producing seeds or fruit? The females are messy (don't blame me, I'm just the reporter). Female ginkgos stink.</p><p>"I honestly don't think it's so horrid," said Horvath. "And a female ginkgo's leaves get this beautiful gold color."</p><p>Planting a male ginkgo is not always a safe bet because — and keep this in mind next time someone starts prattling on about bathrooms — a male ginkgo tree will sometimes switch over to being female, at least a branch or two, if no lady ginkgos are around to pollinate with. Other ginkgos take 20 or 30 years to decide if they're boys or girls. So nature has her thumb on the scale in this debate.</p><p>Back to Arbor Day, an increasingly important celebration of what matters.</p><p>"Other holidays repose upon the past," J. Sterling Morton said. "Arbor Day proposes for the future."</p><p>If we're to have one, we need to start thinking, about trees, and creating a world that nurtures them.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Familia de hombre muerto a tiros por la Policía de Chicago en West Ridge entabla demanda
entries.summary: <p>La familia de un joven de 20 años al que la Policía de Chicago disparó fatalmente el pasado diciembre en el vecindario de West Ridge presentó una demanda por muerte injusta en la corte federal contra la Municipalidad y dos oficiales de policía.</p><p>La familia de Roberto Calvario Jr. alega que “no hizo nada amenazante” cuando dos oficiales se acercaron a su sedán la noche del 9 de diciembre en la cuadra 5700 al norte de Washtenaw Avenue para investigar un informe de actividad “sospechosa” y un posible auto robado.</p><p>Calvario sufrió un disparo en la cabeza y un oficial fue arrastrado brevemente por el sedán antes de que chocara contra un auto estacionado, según la demanda y <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/01/23/hombre-al-que-le-disparo-fatalmente-la-policia-de-chicago-no-representaba-una-amenaza-dicen-abogados" target="_blank" >un video de la cámara corporal</a> del tiroteo.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b00000" name="module-b00000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La demanda fue presentada el miércoles, en lo que hubiera sido el 21 cumpleaños de Calvario, por abogados de Loevy y Loevy, bufete de abogados de derechos civiles con una larga historia de demandas por mala conducta policial.</p><p>El oficial José Salazar disparó antes de ser arrastrado por el sedán, alega la demanda. No está claro, a partir de los videos caóticos de la cámara corporal, cuándo exactamente se disparó el tiro mortal.</p><p>Los oficiales se encontraron con Calvario durante unos 10 segundos antes de que fuera baleado, según la demanda y los videos.</p><p>En los videos se observa a los oficiales corriendo hacia el auto de Calvario y gritando “alto”, mientras otra persona huye. La demanda afirma que el oficial intentó sacar a Calvario del auto y luego le disparó.</p><p>La novia de Calvario estaba en el asiento del pasajero delantero del auto. Un oficial la sacó del auto y la colocó junto a Calvario en la calle nevada mientras los oficiales revisaban a Calvario, según muestra el video.</p><p>Transcurrieron diez minutos antes de que una ambulancia llegara a la escena, mientras Calvario yacía respirando pero sin responder, muestra el video. Calvario murió horas después en un hospital.</p><p>Los oficiales encontraron un arma en la cintura de Calvario y la Oficina Civil de Responsabilidad Policial (COPA) informó que se recuperó una segunda arma. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/McDk321huBY?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Man fatally shot in head by Chicago police 'posed no threat,' family's lawyers say"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>La novia de Calvario fue arrestada y retenida durante la noche en una comisaría, pero no se presentaron cargos, según la demanda, que también alega arresto ilegal.</p><p>La demanda también alega que los oficiales intentaron “encubrir” el tiroteo. Afirmaron, a través de reportes de policía “inexactos y engañosos”, que Calvario los atacó con un arma y que un oficial fue arrastrado por el auto de Calvario, y justificaron supuestamente el disparo como defensa propia.</p><p>Un portavoz del Departamento de Leyes de Chicago dijo el miércoles que la oficina no había sido notificada sobre la demanda y que no comenta sobre litigios pendientes.</p><p>La demanda enumera 14 cargos de presuntas violaciones a los derechos civiles, incluyendo muerte injusta, uso excesivo de la fuerza, arresto falso, imposición de angustia emocional y conspiración.</p><p>“Roberto —un joven que debería haber tenido toda su vida por delante— está muerto porque este oficial le disparó, sin causa aparente, en cuestión de segundos”, declaró Maggie Filler, abogada de Loevy y Loevy, en un comunicado.</p><p>“Nuestros clientes [la madre de Calvario y su novia] han sufrido una pérdida y un trauma que nadie debería tener que soportar”, destacó Filler. “Tienen derecho a exigir justicia y confiamos en que un jurado se la otorgará”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Man, 42, shot during scuffle near Union Station in West Loop
entries.summary: <p>A 42-year-old man wrestled with a gunman who confronted him near Union Station Thursday morning and shot him in the leg, police said. </p><p>The 42-year-old man was in the 300 block of South Canal Street when the attacker confronted him and they began arguing, police said. Union Station is located at 225 S. Canal St.</p><p>The argument turned violent and the attacker pulled a gun and opened fire, hitting the 42-year-old in the left leg, police said. .</p><p>The man was taken in good condition to Stroger Hospital and the shooter fled in a black car. </p><p>Area 3 detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Southern Illinois native Bryan Hudson settling in for White Sox' bullpen
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — <b>Bryan Hudson</b> is no stranger to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a>’ roster shuffle in the bullpen.</p><p>The team added the lanky southern Illinois native late last season, sent him to the Mets and then plucked him off the waiver wire again on opening weekend. Since then, Hudson has settled in as one of manager <b>Will Venable</b>’s most consistent performers in a high-turnover pen.</p><p>“He's been really special,”<b> </b>Venable said before Hudson tossed another scoreless inning Wednesday. The 6-8 lefty has posted a 1.69 ERA in 11 appearances, and hadn’t surrendered a run this month entering the Sox' series finale against the Diamondbacks Thursday.</p><p>“It's all just been working,” said Hudson, who grew up near <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/08/05/bryan-hudson-white-sox-mariners" >downstate Alton</a>. “I’ve been able to be a lot more synced up in my mechanics, and just throwing everything with a lot more confidence.”</p><p>Venable pointed to a rejuvenated four-seam fastball the journeyman perfected in his best season in ‘24 with the Brewers and then-staffer <b>Walker McKinven</b>, now the Sox' bench coach.</p><p>"He always wants to pitch. He wants to be in that big moment," Venable said. "He's been huge for us.”</p><h3><b>Position player pitching</b></h3><p>The Sox’ newest pitcher was a first baseman a few years ago.</p><p>The team called up right-hander <b>Tyler Davis</b>, who was solely a position player in his final season in 2023 at Sam Houston State University in Texas, and figured that was the plan when he went to play for the Oakland Ballers of the Pioneer League the next year.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-portrait-right> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ff0000" name="image-ff0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium-3x4"> <source type="image/webp" width="375" height="500" height="500" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Tyler Davis.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brynn Anderson/AP Photos</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“When I went to independent ball, my pitching coach [<b>Jim Dietrich</b>] there was like, ‘Hey buddy, let’s put the bat down. Let’s focus on pitching and see where that takes you,’” Davis said inside his first big-league clubhouse. “Got three at-bats and he made me stop hitting.”</p><p>The Sox picked him up two weeks later. He pitched to a 3.46 ERA across four levels of the farm system in ‘24, and posted a 3.64 mark in 41 relief appearances with 64 strikeouts and 21 walks last season at Double-A Birmingham.</p><p>Davis, 27, surrendered 4 runs in 7 ⅓ innings before being promoted. “The kind of journey I’ve been on, it’s not the traditional one. So to get that call, it meant everything.”</p><p>He stands ready to step in if <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> needs a break at first.</p><p>“If he ever wants one, I’ll give him a shot,” Davis joked. “I think we have some other guys who’ll do a better job.”</p><h3><b>Mune’s message</b></h3><p>“Being patient, enduring hardship and overcoming obstacles to succeed even in those hardships.”</p><p>They’re words to live by, and wear, for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/22/munetaka-murakami-home-run-fifth-straight-colson-montgomery-miguel-vargas-white-sox" >slugging sensation</a> Murakami, who took heed of the wisdom from a high school teacher and has the Japanese characters stitched on the belt he wears on the field every day.</p><div
