really focus into those words every day.”</p><p>The results have spoken for themselves over Murakami’s first four weeks in big leagues, where the rookie feels like he’s found a supportive home with the Sox.</p><p>“I love the team very much,” he said. “All my teammates are very open to communication. They are really just good teammates overall. Staff, coaches, I love them very much.”</p><h3><b>Coming and going</b></h3><p>The Sox DFA’d reliever <b>Lucas Sims </b>(0-2, 4.50 ERA) to make room for Davis, while left-hander <b>Chris Murphy</b> was scheduled to start a rehab assignment Saturday at Triple-A Charlotte in his return from an elbow injury.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Ya abrieron las inscripciones para campamento de verano del Distrito de Parques y el proceso frustra a algunos
entries.summary: <p>Leah Fagan estaba en su computadora a las 9 a.m. del lunes, justo cuando se abrió la inscripción para los programas de verano del Distrito de Parques de Chicago, para inscribir a su hijo en un popular campamento que sabía que se llenaría rápido.</p><p>Fagan reservó tiempo para el estresante proceso de inscripción anual, incluso sabiendo que llegaría tarde a su trabajo de diseño paisajístico.</p><p>Aproximadamente un minuto después de haber asegurado un lugar para su hijo, todos los lugares estaban llenos.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e90000" name="module-e90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Dijo que el proceso de inscripción del Distrito de Parques funciona para ella, ya que ella o su esposo suelen estar disponibles para conseguir los lugares que se liberan para su hija de 9 años y su hijo de 12.</p><p>Pero el proceso no funciona para todos.</p><p>“Siempre pienso en las familias que, por cualquier razón, no tienen flexibilidad en su horario y simplemente se quedan sin opciones”, dijo Fagan, de 40 años.</p><p>En los últimos años, los padres se han quejado de que las dificultades técnicas y la escasez de cupos les impiden inscribir a sus hijos en los campamentos de verano del distrito, que son algunos de los programas más asequibles de la ciudad. Este año, algunos padres dicen que siguen frustrados a pesar de los cambios del distrito.</p><p>El distrito de parques ha presentado una función de mapa, una página de inscripción renovada y nuevos íconos de "alta demanda" para etiquetar programas populares que probablemente se agoten. El distrito también ha realizado seminarios en línea para responder preguntas previas a la inscripción.</p><p>Fagan dijo que no notó la mayoría de las nuevas características en el sitio web del distrito.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3e9830" name="image-3e9830"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/80fa745/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f4e84ea/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3eb86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Leah Fagan, una madre que inscribió a sus hijos en un campamento de verano del Distrito de Parques de Chicago, se sienta frente a la Escuela Primaria Goethe, ubicada en 2236 N. Rockwell St. en Logan Square." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3eb86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cca7e34/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3eb86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F76%2Fa0655e1747839e6499a017d874d5%2Fparkdistrictcamp-260422-7.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Leah Fagan, una madre que inscribió a sus hijos en un campamento de verano del Distrito de Parques de Chicago, se sienta frente a la Escuela Primaria Goethe, ubicada en 2236 N. Rockwell St. en Logan Square.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Elizabeth Rymut/Archivo Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Si esta no es tu primera vez, entonces probablemente ya sabes cuáles se agotan rápido”, dijo Fagan. “La función de alta demanda no sirvió de mucho de todos modos”.</p><p>Vanessa Lee, de 42 años, inscribió a su hijo de 7 años en un campamento del distrito a principios de esta semana, esperando que pudiera asistir al mismo campamento que sus amigos. Era la primera vez que pasaba por el proceso de inscripción de campamento de verano del distrito en casi cinco años.</p><p>Las cosas no salieron bien.</p><p>Su computadora se congeló con un segundo restante en la cuenta regresiva de la inscripción, dijo. Para cuando el sitio web volvió a funcionar, el campamento preferido de su hijo estaba lleno. Ahora está inscrito en otro campamento y está en una lista de espera para el campamento al que asisten sus amigos.</p><p>“Mi esposo y yo estábamos muy, muy frustrados y molestos de que se colapsara para los dos”, dijo Lee.</p><p>Lee había inscrito a su hijo mayor en actividades de verano del distrito hace unos años y comentó que no recordaba que el proceso de inscripción fuera tan difícil. También dijo que la nueva función de mapa no resolvió ningún desafío porque ella y su esposo solo buscaban parques cercanos.</p><p>“No estoy dispuesta a manejar muy lejos para encontrar un parque que los acepte”, comentó Lee.</p><p>El distrito de parques afirmó que su proceso de inscripción renovado ha llevado a menos quejas.</p><p>“En general, vimos muchas menos llamadas y mensajes en nuestras redes sociales que en años anteriores, lo que refleja el éxito de los seminarios en línea en vivo, la lista de verificación mejorada y nuestra iniciativa para que los padres activen cuentas con anticipación”, dijo Irene Tostado, portavoz del distrito de parques, en un correo electrónico.</p><p>El distrito de parques busca implementar más cambios en el futuro para mejorar el proceso, agregó Tostado.</p><p>Fagan y Lee ambas dijeron que quieren que la inscripción se vuelva un proceso más accesible para las familias que no pueden conectarse a sus computadoras justo a las 9 a.m., cuando típicamente abre la inscripción.</p><p>“Conozco a muchos amigos que no tienen esa flexibilidad, y simplemente buscan en otro lado”, dijo Fagan.</p><p>Los precios de los programas de campamento de verano se mantuvieron relativamente similares a los del año pasado, aunque algunos registraron aumentos significativos. El campamento en Hermitage Park aumentó de $85 a $100. El programa en Wilson Park tuvo el mayor incremento en dólares, al aumentar $45 a una tarifa de $425.</p><p>Shuang Jiang, quien inscribió a sus dos hijas, de 5 y 8 años, en programas de verano del distrito, dijo que el aumento de precios vale la pena.</p><p>“Para ser justos, es mucho más barato que los programas fuera del distrito de parques”, aseguró Jiang, de 40 años. “Creo que todo se está volviendo caro”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: JCPenney to exit Ford City as city pressures mall's owner
entries.summary: <p>The last department store anchor at Ford City Mall is closing, likely sealing the fate of the deteriorating shopping center on the city’s Southwest Side.</p><p>JCPenney said it will close the store in a month or two. In an email, the retailer’s media relations department said, “Despite our best efforts to remain at Ford City Mall, we have been required by the city of Chicago to close this location along with all other remaining tenants at the property, and will be exiting the property in the next 30-60 days. We are actively exploring opportunities for a new store location in the surrounding Chicago area and look forward to sharing updates as plans develop.”</p><p>JCPenney directed shoppers to its nearby Chicago Ridge Mall store, at 95th Street and Ridgeland Avenue.</p><p>Chicago officials have asked Cook County Circuit Court Judge Leonard Murray to order <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/04/17/ford-city-mall-verge-shuttering-namdar-failure-repairs" >Ford City Mall be emptied</a>, citing hazards that include flooding, exposed wiring and a threat of sinkholes.</p><p>A hearing in that case was expected Thursday but was continued until May 15. The city and property owner Namdar Realty Group told the court a defective fire suppression system was fixed.</p><p>Namdar could be forced to sell out or spend heavily on repairs just to reopen Ford City, 7601 S. Cicero Ave. </p><p>Ald. Derrick Curtis (18th) has told constituents that a developer wants to replace the mall with a $150 million complex of warehouses or light manufacturing.</p><p>Curtis said the prospective buyer, Kurv Industrial, is in “waiting status” while the dispute with the city plays out. “I’m hoping that they [Namdar] don’t run them away,” Curtis said. His ward includes Ford City.</p><p>Namdar and Kurv couldn't be reached for comment. City agencies involved in the case declined to comment.</p><p>Curtis said Namdar has neglected the property for years. He’s concerned about flooding that could cause a partial building collapse. Ford City is built over tunnels from when it was a factory for B-29 airplane engines in World War II.</p><p>“You have water that’s going under the foundation. What it’s basically doing is washing away the foundation,” Curtis said.</p><p>The mall is mostly vacant. “It’s a terrible sight,” Curtis said, noting that the few retailers left often close early.</p><p>He has criticized Namdar for letting the site get taken over by “bad elements,” with its parking lots sometimes used for “drifting” takeovers in which drivers spin their wheels and do doughnuts.</p><p>The Ford name dates from when the automaker used the property to build aircraft engines for the Korean War. The site was reborn as a mall in 1965 and once had a Carson’s and Sears.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/04/17/ford-city-mall-verge-shuttering-namdar-failure-repairs" >Ford City Mall on the verge of shuttering after owner fails to make repairs</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: David Roeder

entries.title: Marimar Martínez testifica y exige responsabilidad o 'más personas resultarán heridas'
entries.summary: <p>Marimar Martínez, la mujer de Chicago a la que le disparó un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza en octubre, testificó el miércoles ante el Comité de Seguridad Nacional en Washington, D.C., advirtiendo a los legisladores federales que si no se responsabiliza a los agentes de inmigración, “más personas resultarán heridas”.</p><p>El agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >Charles Exum, le disparó a Martínez, de 31 años, cinco veces</a> después de que un automóvil que transportaba a tres agentes de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) chocara con su vehículo el 4 de octubre cerca de 39th Street y Kedzie Avenue.</p><p>Contó que estaba conduciendo para donar ropa a una iglesia esa mañana cuando notó una camioneta SUV que parecía ser conducida por agentes de inmigración federales que “habían estado invadiendo mi vecindario hispano en las últimas semanas”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-660000" name="module-660000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Después de ver a agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza uniformados en el auto, Martínez dijo que siguió a la SUV, tocó la bocina y gritó “La migra” para alertar a sus vecinos sobre su presencia.</p><p>Su automóvil permaneció “a 2 o 3 pies” a la izquierda del vehículo de la CBP cuando Exum se desvió a su carril. Ella hizo contacto visual con Exum y “observó cómo giraba su volante una vez más hacia la izquierda y chocaba lateralmente contra mi vehículo”, contó.</p><p>“De inmediato me paralicé, pisé el freno y detuve mi auto. La Patrulla Fronteriza se detuvo a sólo uno o dos autos delante de mí”, dijo Martínez. “En ese momento, creí que estaba en peligro basándome en lo que sabía al haber visto la cobertura de noticias sobre los agentes del ICE... en Chicago”.</p><p>Condujo hacia adelante, rodeando a los agentes de CBP mientras uno de ellos le apuntaba con un arma, agregó. Continuó desviándose hacia la izquierda hasta que chocó contra la acera.</p><p>“Sentí sensaciones de ardor en mis brazos y piernas. Poco después, comencé a sentir impactos agudos y una sensación de ardor mientras seguía conduciendo más allá de los agentes de la Patrulla Fronteriza. Pude escuchar cómo estallaba mi ventana trasera”, dijo Martínez. “Miré hacia abajo y noté sangre brotar de mis brazos y piernas, y me di cuenta de que me habían disparado varias veces”.</p><p>El miércoles fue la primera vez que Martínez testificó ante un comité. La acompañó el reverendo David Black, de la Primera Iglesia Presbiteriana de Chicago, quien testificó sobre cuando los agentes del ICE le dispararon bolas de gas pimienta mientras rezaba afuera de la instalación de procesamiento de ICE en Broadview.</p><p>“Lo que me hicieron es solo un reflejo de lo que se está haciendo a las personas en mi comunidad que no tienen púlpito ni plataforma”, dijo Black. “A diario, presencié el miedo y el sufrimiento de aquellos que están siendo blanco de esta campaña violenta”.</p><p>George Retes Jr., ciudadano estadounidense que fue gaseado y arrestado por el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) mientras se dirigía a trabajar en el sur de California el julio pasado, y Ryan Ecklund, otro ciudadano estadounidense detenido por ICE en enero en Minnesota, también testificaron en la audiencia del miércoles.</p><p>Martínez apareció por primera vez ante el Congreso en un <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-testifica-en-washington-pide-a-funcionarios-que-dejen-de-llamarla-terrorista-domestica" target="_blank" >foro público del 3 de febrero</a> organizado en respuesta a las muertes de Renee Macklin Good y Alex Pretti a manos de agentes de inmigración federales en Minnesota. Les dijo a los legisladores entonces que quería que se responsabilizara a los agentes federales, quería escuchar una disculpa y quería que la administración de Trump reconociera que no es “una terrorista doméstica”.</p><p>Ella reiteró eso el miércoles, afirmando principalmente que ella y “nosotros, los estadounidenses” queremos honestidad, transparencia y responsabilidad de la administración de Trump.</p><p>“Miren lo que le pasó a Renee [Macklin Good], miren lo que le pasó a Alex Pretti. Está destinado a suceder más temprano que tarde si no responsabilizamos a estos agentes por sus acciones. Más personas resultarán heridas”, puntualizó Martínez.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="150" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/xmg8n9mcvzA?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Funding Lapse & Security Gaps: Assessing Harmful Impacts of the DHS Shutdown on Americans, Part II"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>La audiencia del miércoles se llevó a cabo a petición de los demócratas del comité, quienes también solicitaron la comparecencia del jefe de personal adjunto de la Casa Blanca, Stephen Miller, y del “zar de la frontera”, Tom Homan. Ninguno de ellos se presentó. De los 17 miembros republicanos del comité, sólo uno —el presidente Andrew Garbarino, republicano por Nueva York— asistió el miércoles.</p><p>La audiencia del Comité de Seguridad Nacional se convocó para discutir el impacto del cierre del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) que ahora ha durado 67 días, el cierre parcial del gobierno más largo en la historia. Los demócratas del Senado han bloqueado el financiamiento de DHS y están pidiendo reformas de las tácticas del ICE y de CBP.</p><p>Cuando fue interrogada por el representante Al Green, demócrata por Texas, Martínez dijo que piensa en el tiroteo de octubre “todo el tiempo”, ha tenido pesadillas y “a veces” todavía ha perdido sueño. En un momento, se levantó, se quitó la chamarra y les mostró a los legisladores dónde le dispararon.</p><p>Martínez señaló que después de ser herida, continuó conduciendo alrededor de una milla hasta un taller mecánico, donde los trabajadores le ayudaron y llamaron al 911.</p><p>“Imagínense si me hubiera quedado [allí]. No estaría aquí para testificar. No estaría”, dijo Martínez.</p><p>Exum, aparentemente, se jactó ante otros agentes de la CBP de haberle disparado a Martínez. En un mensaje de texto previamente mostrado en la corte, escribió: “Disparé 5 balas y ella tenía 7 agujeros. Anoten eso, muchachos”.</p><p>Martínez dijo el miércoles que “no es justo” que a ella se le haya presentado cargos en relación con el tiroteo mientras que Exum no ha sido responsabilizado.</p><p>“He ido y regresado del infierno, y he luchado por mi libertad, he luchado por mi voz”, dijo Martínez. “Y estos agentes, Charles Exum ni siquiera ha sido responsabilizado por sus acciones. Pero cuando se trata de mí, tuve que luchar. ¿Y él qué?”</p><p>Hacia el final de la audiencia de aproximadamente 1½ horas, Martínez dijo que quiere que se presenten cargos y se juzgue a Exum por el tiroteo.</p><p>“Quiero que el mundo vea mi dolor, mi trauma, porque esto no es algo de lo que se deba bromear, ¿cierto?”, dijo Martínez. “Esta es mi vida. Estamos hablando de mi vida”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: United Airlines considers raising ticket prices 15% to 20% to offset fuel costs due to war in Iran
entries.summary: <p>United Airlines is considering raising ticket prices by 15% to 20% to offset the price of jet fuel that has spiked during the U.S.-Israel war with Iran.</p><p>The Chicago-based company is seeking to recoup the higher cost of fuel "as quickly as possible," United CEO Scott Kirby said in a Wednesday earnings call.</p><p>"To recover 100% of fuel costs, yields need to increase by about 15% to 20%, and we are assuming that fuel may remain higher for longer," Kirby said.</p><p>The cost of fuel has remained elevated during the war with Iran, and the disruption to oil tankers passing through the Straight of Hormuz off Iran's coast.</p><p>But ticket prices on United have already been increasing.</p><p>The company reported ticket yields rose 12% year-over-year in early March. That rose to 18% later that month.</p><p>Fuel prices are expected to average around $4.30 per gallon in the current quarter, the company reported.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Party City making comeback inside Staples
entries.summary: <p>Party goods retailer Party City reopened inside more than 700 Staples office supply stores nationwide, including eight in Illinois.</p><p>Party City is open at the Staples in the Loop at 111 N. Wabash Ave. Other Illinois locations include Glen Ellyn, Glenview and Shorewood, according to Staples’ website.<b> </b>It plans to bring Party City to more stores by the end of this year, the retailer said in a news release on Thursday.</p><p>In 2024, Party City announced it would <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/12/20/party-city-shut-stores-chicago-nationwide" target="_blank" >shut down</a> after filing for bankruptcy the previous year. The New Jersey-based chain previously had more than 20 locations in the Chicago area.</p><p>When looking for a partner to revive Party City, “Staples stood out immediately, not only for its national footprint, but for its strong connection to the communities we have both been part of for years,” Joel Weinshanker, CEO of Ad Populum, owner of Party City, said in a news release.</p><p>“By bringing Party City into Staples stores, we’re expanding what customers can accomplish in one place — combining helium balloons and party supplies with our print and marketing services,” Marshall Warkentin, president of Staples U.S. Retail, said in a news release.</p><p>Party City will return to selling party décor and favors, tableware, gift bags, costume accessories and more, while Staples’ print services will offer banners, yard signs, posters and personalized invitations.</p><p>In the coming weeks, customers will be able to schedule balloon pickups through Staples.com and the Staples app.</p><p>The growth of e-commerce and pop-up concepts like Spirit Halloween hurt Party City’s business and were factors that led to its closure. Party City was known for selling Halloween costumes and balloons.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/12/20/party-city-shut-stores-chicago-nationwide" >Party City’s party is over, shutting stores nationally, including more than 20 Chicago locations</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Larry Zgoda, stained-glass artist who helped preserve legacy of artistic genius Edgar Miller, dies at 75
entries.summary: <p></p><p></p><p></p><p>Stained-glass artist Larry Zgoda loved the unusual, whimsical and wacky.</p><p>While exploring Old Town in the early 1980s, he was drawn to several buildings, such as Carl Street Studios and the Walter Guest Apartments, that boasted murals, woodcarvings, stained glass and metalwork like he'd never before seen.</p><p>He asked around,and learned they were creations of Edgar Miller, a celebrated Chicago artist and designer in the '20s and '30s who had faded into obscurity after World War II.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda read everything he could on Miller, asked around some more, and learned Miller was still alive and living in California.</p><p>He wrote Miller a letter, beginning a yearlong correspondence that led to their first meeting in 1984 and an agreement to collaborate on creating a stained-glass window for a Chicago church. It was the first of many such collaborations between the two, with Miller designing and Mr. Zgoda fabricating. Miller even moved back to Chicago.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda felt Miller was a talent on the level of a Frank Lloyd Wright, but without the accompanying recognition. So he began cataloging and archiving information on Miller, who died in 1993.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda helped found <a class="Link" href="https://www.edgarmiller.org/" target="_blank" >Edgar Miller Legacy</a>, a non-profit that seeks to preserve Miller's work.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Larry Zgoda (left) with Edgar Miller</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Edgar Miller Legacy</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"If it weren't for Larry, the world wouldn't know the story and work of Edgar," said Zac Bleicher, executive director of the group.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda's research provided the framework for the book “<a class="Link" href="https://www.cityfilespress.com/books/edgar-miller-and-the-handmade-home/" target="_blank" >Edgar Miller and the Handmade Home: Chicago’s Forgotten Renaissance Man,</a>” which was written by Richard Cahan and Michael Williams and published in 2009.</p><p>"I was amazed that Larry, an amazing artist in his own right, was so willing to share all this research he'd done without any reservations, how selfless he was about putting Miller in the spotlight and not taking any credit," Cahan said.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda died Feb. 9 from cancer. He was 75.</p><p>"There was beauty in the built environment threaded throughout Larry's life, and after he met Edgar he really flourished," said Mr. Zgoda's wife, Joan Zgoda.</p><p>He opened Larry Zgoda Studio on the Northwest Side in 1978 after studying film at Columbia College Chicago and learning how to work with glass largely on his own.</p><p>"He was a fun-loving artist, man. He thought outside the box," said Mark Radina, who used help Mr. Zgoda fabricate stained glass. "We'd be cracking jokes all the time listening to many varieties of music, from the Ramones through classical and Edith Piaf. It was just really fun working with him."</p><p>Early in Mr. Zgoda's career, he created a few knockoffs to pay the bills, but he quickly found that it didn't satisfy him. He later devoted himself to proving that stained glass was a living art, fully adaptable to contemporary architecture.</p><p>"I hate to say it, but almost everybody who works in my craft is replicating Frank Lloyd Wright, replicating Tiffany, replicating Victorian-era stuff," Mr. Zgoda told the Sun-Times in 2006. "Anybody can do that — it's paint-by-numbers. What I want to do is create things that never existed before."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" Podgorski/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Among Mr. Zgoda's largest and most striking architectural installations are his doors and windows for buildings at a convent in Lemont, a synagogue in Evanston, the Clearing and Logan Square branches of the Chicago Public Library, an office tower in Minneapolis and the University of Denver's Graduate School of Social Work.</p><p>"He looked like a middle-class Chicago guy, but that's not the way he thought. Wrapped up in the middle of him was this alternate universe," Cahan said.</p><p>Unique stained-glass windows and standalone pieces, often displayed on easels he built and priced from several hundred to several thousand dollars, can be found in homes and collections around Chicago.</p><p>"Larry was endlessly curious. Gentle. Kind. Creative. Generous. And so unassuming despite his own significant body of work as well as important collaborations with Edgar," said friend and architect P.K. "Trish" VanderBeke.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda was born Oct 7, 1950, and grew up in Bucktown and Arlington Heights. His father, Alexander, was a mailman and a woodworker. His mother, Augustina, was a seamstress.</p><p>Mr. Zgoda most recently lived in Woodstock but for many years lived near Irving Park Road and Western Avenue, not far from his North Side studio.</p><p>"It was pretty cool walking into his studio, there would be rows and rows of glass and all kinds of jewels and metal and other cool stuff, and Larry was always so willing to talk about art and design," Bleicher said. </p><p>Services have been held.<br></p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Shooting prompts 'soft lockdown' at Evanston Township high school; no one injured
entries.summary: <p>The public is urged to avoid the area around Evanston Township High School which went on "precautionary soft lockdown" Thursday afternoon when a shooting erupted at a park nearby, according to police.</p><p>No one was injured during the shooting at Mason Park, which is located at Florence and Mason avenues, according to police. Evanston high, 1600 Dodge Ave., is around the corner. </p><p>"Please avoid the area. This is an active investigation," police said on X. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Former owner of the “Home Alone” house dies by suicide, police say
entries.summary: <p>The man who once owned the Winnetka house where “Home Alone” was filmed was found dead, apparently by suicide, Wednesday at a nature preserve, according to Lake Forest police.</p><p>John Abendshien, 79, was reported missing by his wife at 3:50 p.m. Wednesday, police said. Lake Forest officials found him nearly two hours later using drones and a police dog.</p><p> Abendshien was found dead at Derwen Mawr Nature Preserve behind a residence in the 300 block of West Westminster Avenue, according to officials.</p><p>Autopsy results indicated that Abendshien died from severe oxygen deprivation that appeared to have been self-inflicted. The circumstances surrounding the death remain under investigation by the Lake Forest Police Department and the Lake County coroner’s office.</p><p>The Lake Forest Police Department took Abendshien into custody April 16 after discovering the previous “Home Alone” homeowner had engaged in possession, manufacture or distribution of child sexual abuse material.</p><p>Abendshien was released after his hearing but that investigation continues.<br></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Can't-lose Cubs sweep Phillies for ninth straight 'W,' majors' longest streak this season
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a> have a ritual of sorts after wins this season, one during which the clubhouse floor is open.</p><p>From their stalls in the circular locker room, players — any of them — can shout out teammates for things done well. Some days, there are more of those things than others. Lately, there have been too many to count.</p><p>The shout-out list was long Thursday after an 8-7, 10-inning walk-off victory against the Phillies that sent the Cubs westward on a nine-game winning streak, the longest in the majors this season.</p><p>“Even if it takes five minutes after the game, I think that’s what brings this group close,” catcher Carson Kelly said.</p><p>Close? That’s what you want.</p><p>Hot? It’s getting ridiculous.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/23/cubs-craig-counsell-halfway-to-hall-of-fame-cooperstown-baseball-mlb" target="_blank" >Halfway to the Hall? Cubs’ Craig Counsell’s mini-milestone is reason to wonder</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/22/cubs-win-eighth-straight-behind-matthew-boyds-return-ben-browns-relief-work" >Cubs win eighth straight behind Matthew Boyd’s return, Ben Brown’s relief work</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/04/22/wrigley-rat-spooks-phillies-amuses-cubs-fans" >Wrigley rat spooks Phillies, annoys Cubs brass</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The pitchers — in particular the starters — are on fire, with the Cubs (16-9) posting the majors’ lowest ERA since the start of the winning streak on April 14.</p><p>The offense is in binge mode, piling up 66 runs (with a plus-39 differential) over that span. Double-digit hit totals — the Cubs had 18 on Thursday — have become the norm.</p><p>After slow starts, right fielder Seiya Suzuki and first baseman Michael Busch are getting back to wielding the lineup’s most explosive power. Suzuki, who homered to left in the eighth inning for a 7-6 lead, had three hits and has now gone deep in three straight games for the second time in his career. Busch, whose three-run shot off Christopher Sanchez in the third was the swing of the game until Dansby Swanson gapped one in the 10th to end it, has homered in back-to-back games.</p><p>Ian Happ also homered three times on the Cubs’ first 7-0 homestand since 2015 and leads the team with seven thus far. Hitting from the right side, Happ lit up a fourth-inning slider off Sanchez and deposited it onto Waveland Ave.</p><p>The nine-game overall streak — all of it against the Phillies and Mets, who were supposed to be good but instead have the worst records in the National League — is the Cubs’ first since the 2016 World Series winners pulled off an 11-gamer. A four-game sweep of the Phillies (8-17) was the first by a Cubs team since 1973.</p><p>“You can say they’re struggling, but hopefully we’re part of that,” manager Craig Counsell said. “That’s what I would like to say, that we’ve made it tough on them.”</p><p>With three games upcoming against the Dodgers followed by three against the Padres, the Cubs are about to step up in competition. Good news: At Dodger Stadium, they’ll avoid starting pitchers Shohei Ohtani, Yoshinobu Yamamoto and Tyler Glasnow. Luck like that never hurts.</p><p>“I think it’s always a good time to go there,” second baseman Nico Hoerner said. "It’s one of the great venues in our sport, you get great crowds and it’s always exciting to see how you stack up with another good team.”</p><p>The Cubs’ defense has been hot, too, and it was again in this game — with sparkling plays by Swanson, Busch and Hoerner and an impressive pounce on a bunt by long-legged starting pitcher Edward Cabrera — until things fell apart in the seventh. Suzuki couldn’t squeeze a ball in his glove on a reasonably difficult play in deep right, then third baseman Alex Bregman fielded a grounder and threw wildly to first, leading to two unearned runs.</p><p>And yet another injury bit the Cubs’ pitching staff, this time with lefty reliever Caleb Thielbar — one of the team’s most leaned-on players to this point — having to exit the game in the ninth with tightness in his left hamstring. He’ll be evaluated further on Friday.</p><p>But Riley Martin came into a 7-7 game and kept it there, and Javier Assad found what he needed to get the Cubs through the top of the 10th.</p><p>“It’s a complete group,” Happ said. “We’ve done it in different ways. Wind blowing out, wind blowing in, great defensive days, great pitching performances, and the offense has been exceptional.”</p><p>That’s a whole lot of good going on. The Cubs deserve a shout-out, no doubt.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Chicago area bookstores are set to host their biggest celebration yet for Independent Bookstore Day
entries.summary: <p>Despite pressure from competitors like Barnes & Noble and Amazon, Chicago’s indie bookstores are preparing for the biggest Indie Bookstore Day yet.</p><p>Book lovers will have the chance to explore local shops across the Chicago area during Saturday's annual <a class="Link" href="https://www.chilovebooks.com/independent-bookstore-day.html" target="_blank" >Chicagoland Bookstore Crawl</a>, which will be held alongside the national Independent Bookstore Day.</p><p>All 84 participating stores offer their own specialties and curate selections that reflect the interests of their neighborhoods, said Ryvre Hardrick, organizer of the crawl and owner of Goblin Market in Lake View.</p><p>The number of participating shops nearly doubled from last year, a shift Hardrick said reflects owners’ desire to keep building connections with their neighbors as well as growing interest in “analog hobbies.”</p><p>“We focus on being a true third space for our neighbors and communities, and making sure that the books we're selecting really represent what our communities are looking for and the very diverse views of our community,” Hardrick said.</p><p>Bookstore owners said their mission has always been to create spaces where their communities can gather, and as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2026/01/15/volumes-bookcafe-wicker-park-close" target="_blank" ><u>larger retailers move into their neighborhoods</u></a>, they are doubling down on those efforts.</p><p>Hardrick said people were “hungry” for screen-free, offline activities — something physical books naturally provide.</p><p>“It’s a really good time for reading, a really good time for books,” Hardrick said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/559cad6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F26%2F959becb5446fbbfa47275adaacde%2Fblackbookstores-01xx25-40.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b0d5055/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F26%2F959becb5446fbbfa47275adaacde%2Fblackbookstores-01xx25-40.jpg data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1bb104d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F26%2F959becb5446fbbfa47275adaacde%2Fblackbookstores-01xx25-40.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c25622a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F26%2F959becb5446fbbfa47275adaacde%2Fblackbookstores-01xx25-40.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1bb104d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F66%2F26%2F959becb5446fbbfa47275adaacde%2Fblackbookstores-01xx25-40.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Courtney Bledsoe owner of Call & Response Books sits in her Hyde Park bookstore holding one of her favorite books “Wash Day Diaries” by Jamila Rowser and Robyn Smith, on Jan. 29, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Meanwhile, large retailers like Barnes & Noble are continuing to expand in the Chicago area following what they’ve described as “successful” store openings. The company was also expected to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2026/01/08/barnes-noble-largest-chicago-bookstore-successful-openings" target="_blank" >open its largest location</a> to date in the Loop in June.</p><p>But small business owners said those retailers can’t “replicate” what independent bookstores offer, because they do more than “just sell books.”</p><p>“We are passionate about what we sell in our stores and we care about our community,” said Hardrick, whose store specializes in manga and comic books.</p><p>About 3,000 people participated in the crawl last year. Owners are expecting an even larger crowd this time around.</p><p>Courtney Bledsoe, owner of Call and Response Books in Hyde Park, said they saw about 700 people stop by the store during last year’s crawl. Participating once again was a no-brainer, she said.</p><p>“We're just excited for it because it's a great way to raise visibility about independent bookstores and the importance of them in our communities,” said Bledsoe, who opened her store in 2024.</p><p>Call and Response is one of few <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/books/2025/02/17/black-owned-bookstores-in-chicago-struggle-after-2020-surge-as-if-supporting-us-was-a-trend" target="_blank" >Black-owned bookstores</a> in Chicago that focuses on stocking books by authors of color.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe src="https://flo.uri.sh/visualisation/28670476/embed" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" height="575" width="700" style="width:100%;" title="Interactive or visual content"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Like other bookstores, Bledsoe said what has kept her business going is giving the neighborhood a place to gather. She said the store hosts book clubs, trivia nights and concerts.</p><p>Blendsoe said the bookstore crawl would give participants an opportunity to learn more about what businesses like her have to offer.</p><p>Shoppers who stop by more than 10 stores during the crawl will receive 10% off purchases on all participating bookstores valid until next year’s bookstore crawl. Those who visit more than 15 stores, will receive 15% off their purchases.</p><p>Participants can pick up a “passport” at the first store and get it stamped at every store during the crawl.</p><p>Bookstores also will have planned activities during the crawl including raffles and live music and will even offer some discounts.</p><p>Hardrick said they hoped the crawl would give community members a chance not only to get to know one another, but also to inspire them to continue supporting small businesses.</p><p>“We’re committed to giving our neighbors a place where they can be themselves,” Hardrick said.<br></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Munetaka Murakami's homer streak ends, but Andrew Benintendi's blast seals red-hot White Sox' win
entries.summary: <p>PHOENIX — The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>' unprecedented home run streaks are over, but their momentum was at a season-high in the desert on Thursday.</p><p>Absent a home run from Munetaka Murakami, Colson Montgomery or Miguel Vargas for the first time in almost a week, it was Andrew Benintendi carrying the torch for a rejuvenated offense. </p><p>The veteran's three-run, ninth inning homer off Diamondbacks closer Paul Sewald capitalized on another stellar outing from Sox starter Davis Martin, sealed a series win and put the exclamation mark on an invigorating 4-2 road trip for a team that scuffled out of the gate but suddenly has the hottest offense in baseball.</p><p>"Start of the year, everybody is struggling. You see a bunch of .100 batting averages up on the board and you start to panic. Nobody in here panics," Benintendi said. "When you are feeling good, take advantage of it because it won’t last forever."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">FOR THE LEAD! 💪 <a href="https://t.co/wkknv8STfD">pic.twitter.com/wkknv8STfD</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2047437904075518458?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 23, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Murakami, Montgomery and Vargas had taken full advantage throughout the West Coast swing, with the first baseman homering in a franchise-record five straight games, the shortstop in four straight and the third baseman in three — the first time in MLB history that a trio of teammates ever went on such torrid streaks concurrently.</p><p>While the trio's dinger bonanza concluded — just barely for Vargas, who had a ninth-inning fly fall a few feet shy of the Chase Field stands — they each tagged singles, including three for Montgomery. </p><p>Vargas still got the Sox on the board with his RBI single in the third inning, while Murakami struck out three times but also made a diving stop in the field.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Munetaka Murakami flashing the leather! <a href="https://t.co/XcpCvqeYQr">pic.twitter.com/XcpCvqeYQr</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2047420141256724978?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 23, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>"They’re just so connected. We’ve seen that throughout the year as we’ve continued to play better and better," manager Will Venable said. "That confidence just grows."</p><p>After getting tagged for an RBI double in the first inning, Martin further bolstered his status as <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/16/white-sox-davis-martin-shane-smith-martin-perez-shea-langeliers-athletics" >the staff’s de facto ace</a>, trading zeroes with D-backs starter and former Sox pitcher Michael Soroka and the Arizona bullpen. Venable gave him the hook in the seventh inning after Marting gave up the sixth hit he'd sprinkled over 6 1/3 innings with a walk and seven strikeouts. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-750000" name="image-750000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/728e34a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4091x2296+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F21%2Fbd%2F3829acb84737ab8170a5638368b0%2F2272821760.jpg" alt="Davis Martin pitches against the Arizona Diamondbacks Thursday." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/728e34a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4091x2296+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F21%2Fbd%2F3829acb84737ab8170a5638368b0%2F2272821760.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c0a134/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4091x2296+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F21%2Fbd%2F3829acb84737ab8170a5638368b0%2F2272821760.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/728e34a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4091x2296+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F21%2Fbd%2F3829acb84737ab8170a5638368b0%2F2272821760.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Davis Martin pitches against the Arizona Diamondbacks Thursday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Christian Petersen/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"I was still just kind of going through the motions, and kind of got punched in the face a little bit and just responded really well," Martin said. "Where I'm at mentally is just going out there and competing the best I can and trusting your defense."</p><p>Grant Taylor entered and gave up a double, but struck his way out of the jam, earning a dugout kiss from a thankful Martin. "I was going to go kiss [him] on the lips, and he pulled away," the starter joked. "We love celebrating each other's successes and love having fun together."</p><p>Taylor pitched a clean eighth inning before Benintendi's blast, which sailed 410 feet and carried a whole lot of momentum with the team back to Chicago. </p><p>The Sox boosted their OPS by 100 points while scoring 44 runs and slugging 16 home runs during the six-game trip. Most of those came from the heart of the order in Vargas, Montgomery and Murakami, who put himself in the national spotlight <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/22/mune-ball-murakami-white-sox-home-runs-offense-colson-montgomery" >with the five-game streak</a>.</p><p>"Last year we showed after the break that a couple of guys get it going, it seems like everybody else does too," Benintendi said. "One goes, we all go."</p><p>The on-field outburst has been a treat for Venable, but first, he pointed off the field.</p><p>“For me, it's what's going on in the clubhouse and these hitter meetings and how these guys are connected and just creating the connection and vibe,” Venable said. “When you're scoring runs, it's easier to do that. But it's some of the things that we saw even before we started scoring runs, the way that they communicate with each other, the way that [hitting coach Derek Shomon] runs those meetings, and guys are talking about their approaches and sharing ideas and then going out here and executing it.”</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: Angel Reese learns to thrive while ignoring negativity
entries.summary: <p>WNBA star Angel Reese won't be deterred by negativity.</p><p>Reese, a two-time All-Star while with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" >Sky,</a> found a new home with the Atlanta Dream after she was traded during the offseason. The trade comes after what Reese describes as some highs (winning a championship at LSU and being a top-10 WNBA Draft pick) and lows (learning now to navigate the narratives around her transition to the league) over the last two years.</p><p>The Dream forward says that the negativity she experienced wasn't normal or acceptable. Still, she understands it's her "new reality," and she's learning how to be grateful and graceful through her experiences.</p><p>"I'm getting pulled by the wins, but I'm also getting pulled by the losses, and how do I find a medium where I'm able to just cope with both?" Reese said on the "IMO" podcast with former First Lady Michelle Obama and her brother, Craig Robinson.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/cxSH74qD3_M?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="ANGEL REESE on the WNBA, Reality TV, and Establishing Her Legacy | IMO"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>"Everybody always asks me, like, 'Are you alright?' Like, yeah, I'm alright as I can be, and everything is not great, but I try to wake up every day and just be grateful for every moment. Every moment hasn't been great, but I wouldn't change this life for anything because I've had some really good moments. And I don't want bad moments to outshine the good moments ... I try not to let the negative outshine the positive."</p><p>Reese says that amid what was, at times, a challenging period, after leaving college and playing in Chicago, she still achieved the goals she wanted: continuing to have a successful basketball career, starting a foundation and having a signature shoe. The 23-year-old also said that she continues to push forward with the mindset that everything, even small things, is a win for her.</p><p>"I don't even let the negativity get to me. It doesn't bother me. You see it. I see it. We all see it ... I've grown to have a skin where you can't break me. You just can't break me," Reese said.</p><p>"It just makes me go harder. I have so many goals. It's like if I stop now, I proved you right, and I proved you right. And I have nothing to prove to anybody but myself. And I just know, like, I want to continue to do the things that I want to do. Nobody can – I'm not going to let the outside control my own narrative of what I want to be and what I wanna do."</p><p><i>Read more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://www.usatoday.com" target="_blank" ><i><u>usatoday.com</u></i></a></p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-010001" name="list-010001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> Latest on the Sky and WNBA </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListH-items" data-with-borders data-list-loadmore-items> <div class="PageListH-items-item"><div class="PagePromoB"> <div class="PagePromo-media"><a class="Link" aria-label="Will this time be different? entries.author: USA TODAY

entries.title: Illinois marijuana firms will get a massive tax break from Trump administration's historic weed shift
entries.summary: <p>The Trump administration's reclassification of medical marijuana will offer some Illinois weed firms a massive tax break, but industry leaders say more action is needed for comprehensive reform. </p><p>Acting U.S. Attorney General Todd Blanche signed an order that shifts state-licensed medical cannabis from a Schedule I — meant for drugs without medical use and a high potential for abuse — to the more loosely regulated Schedule III. It also applies to cannabis products approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.</p><p>The policy shift offers medical marijuana operators significant tax relief by allowing companies to deduct business expenses on their federal taxes. It also lowers the barrier for more cannabis research.</p><p>George Archos, founder and CEO of Verano, which operates 10 Zen Leaf dispensaries in the Chicago area and several more in the Midwest and Florida, said the company would save an estimated $80 million a year from the tax break.</p><p>“We could invest back into our operations, putting us on a level playing field with any other legal business,” Archos said.</p><p>Recreational cannabis remains a Schedule I drug, but Blanche’s order sets up an expedited Drug Enforcement Administration hearing process beginning June 29 to consider broader reclassification to Schedule III.</p><p>Rescheduling medical cannabis is a “long-overdue step that finally brings federal policy closer to the science,” said Charlie Bachtell, CEO of Cresco Labs, which operates 10 Sunnyside dispensaries in Illinois.</p><p>“This decision opens the door to expanded research, better data, more informed care, and broader access for those who need it most,” Bachtell said in a statement. “It is a win for all patients nationwide and will usher in a new era of healthcare, where cannabis is treated like any other medicine.”</p><p>The differing classifications for the same substance breaks a longstanding stance in the Controlled Substances Act, said Mike Feldman, general counsel for Nabis, the largest cannabis distributor in the U.S.</p><p>“[The order] creates a regime where the same product, in the same packaging, is federally Schedule III or Schedule I, depending on where it is purchased,” Feldman said. “That has never been how drug schedules work, for opioids, for psychedelics, for anything."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3b0000" name="image-3b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb8af92/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c421fd3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0280a83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="WEEDSCHEDULEIII-260424-02.JPG" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0280a83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/74c97bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0280a83/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F62%2F51%2F1c6305fc4a598e0b8fca4803f99e%2Fweedscheduleiii-260424-02.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An employee hands a customer their order at Zen Leaf Dispensary at 222 S Halsted St at Greektown, Thursday, April 23, 2026. Trump’s acting AG signed an order reclassifying state-licensed medical marijuana as a Schedule III drug, giving medical marijuana operators a tax break and lowering the barrier to more research.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But even reclassifying all types of cannabis isn’t going far enough to reform cannabis laws, said Betty Aldworth, chair of the Marijuana Policy Project.</p><p>“It does not resolve the fundamental contradictions between federal and state law. It does not address the ongoing reality of cash-only operations that put workers and communities at risk. It does not protect people from the legal consequences of cannabis use embedded in housing, immigration, employment, or family law,” Aldworth said in a statement.</p><p>“And it does not create a pathway for patients to access cannabis through insurance coverage, even when it may be safer or more effective than legal options.”</p><p>Kevin Sabet, president of Smart Approaches to Marijuana, which has been opposed to cannabis reclassification and legalization, called Blanche’s order an “injustice to the American people of unprecedented proportions” because of the massive tax breaks. He argued the move does nothing for cannabis research.</p><p>“What we would need for research is funding… And funding is not in any of these orders,” Sabet said.</p><p>He said the reclassification without further research reminds him of Big Tobacco putting profits over public health.</p><p>“It’s a massive industry that relies on addiction for profit and that’s what my worry is if we allow this to happen,” Sabet said.</p><p>All cannabis use remains federally illegal, though 24 states plus Washington D.C. have legalized adult recreational marijuana use, 40 allow medical marijuana use, and eight others allow low-THC cannabis or CBD oil for medical use. Only Idaho and Kansas ban marijuana outright.</p><p>Illinois has around 50,000 monthly medical marijuana patients, which is down from more than 70,000 in 2021 but is roughly the same as when the state legalized recreational use in 2020, state data shows.</p><p>The state collected $13 million in medical marijuana sales in February, down from $20 million at the same time last year and from its high of around $35 million collected monthly in 2021, according to state data. Illinois sold $105 million in recreational cannabis in February.</p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Illinois House punts millionaires' tax to 2028, advances voting rights amendment
entries.summary: <p>Despite a late push by top state Democrats, the Illinois legislature will pass on a millionaires' tax amendment until at least 2028.</p><p>The tax would have raked in about $4 billion a year by tacking a 3% surcharge on personal income over $1 million. Revenue generated from the tax would have been split 50/50 between public school funding and property tax relief. House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch, D-Hillside, said they were “very close” to having the votes.</p><p>“What we heard from listening to a lot of people over the last few days is that more work needs to be done,” said Welch, who emphasized a need to bring more business-focused representatives on board with the measure.</p><p>Because the Illinois Constitution enshrines a flat tax system into law, the millionaires' tax would have needed approval from voters. The deadline for the Legislature to put amendments on the 2026 November midterm ballot is May 3, and while the Senate is back in session next week, the House won’t reconvene until May 4. </p><p>Welch said a millionaires' tax will be revisited, leaving 2028 as the soonest option.</p><p> Jackson Potter, vice president of the Chicago Teachers Union, slammed Springfield for failing to give more funding to schools while also approving a megaprojects bill, which the union is concerned will cause it to lose out on funding from property taxes.</p><p>"Budgets are documents rooted in morals and math,” Potter said. “The governor and the General Assembly are currently failing at both."</p><p>House Republicans were relieved that the amendment did not go through.</p><p>"You do not solve government mismanagement by writing permanent expansion of higher taxes into the Constitution," House Minority Leader Tony McCombie, R-Savanna, said. "You solve it with discipline, reform and accountability.”</p><p>The wealth tax proposed this go-around was a watered-down version of a failed 2020 attempt to switch the state's tax system to a graduated income tax. Voters showed support for a millionaires' tax during a 2024 nonbinding referendum that passed with over 60% support.</p><p>While the millionaires' tax failed, the House took steps to put a new state voting rights amendment on the ballot instead. Passing with support from Democrats and opposition from Republicans, it now heads to the Senate.</p><p>The amendment places parts of the federal Voting Rights Act into state law by preventing legislative or congressional districts from being drawn to discriminate based on race or excessively compact voter populations. Democratic supporters pushed the bill, concerned the U.S. Supreme Court might tank sections of the Voting Rights Act in a case it heard late last year.</p><p>“Here in Illinois, we refuse to go back,” said Welch, who sponsored the amendment. </p><p>House Republicans pushed back, citing how current maps are already drawn to heavily favor Illinois Democrats. Some Republicans asked for an amendment to prevent politically motivated redistricting. </p><p>“Diversity is important, it truly is,” said Rep. Ryan Spain, R-Peoria. “Racial diversity, ethnic diversity, gender diversity, but also political diversity, and that is not represented in this body and in this state.”</p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Illinois House speaker staying mum on fellow lawmaker ousted from House Democratic caucus
entries.summary: <p>Illinois House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch late Wednesday balked at discussing the circumstances behind the ouster of state Rep. Harry Benton from the House Democratic caucus, citing a desire to "protect victims." </p><p>In late February, Benton, a Democrat from Plainfield, was barred from House Democratic caucus meetings and stripped of his committee assignments. Since then, Welch has remained tight-lipped on any details related to the reasons for Benton’s ouster. </p><p>After being booted from his assignments, Benton didn’t appear in Springfield for some time. He has returned in recent weeks, however, casting votes as hundreds of bills have moved through the House. </p><p>Pressed by reporters in Springfield late Wednesday to explain why he wouldn’t comment on Benton’s potential resignation, Welch said “people are entitled to due process,” and that information will come out at the “proper time."</p><p>“There is a process in place that guarantees every one of us due process, and we're going to allow that process to play out,” Welch said. “I think part of that is not coming out and speaking to the press.</p><p>“We also have to talk about victims and what they're entitled to. Coming out and talking to reporters about a process that's ongoing is not the proper thing to do — and so we're going to respect due process, we're going to respect victims, we're going to respect people's rights.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4e0000" name="image-4e0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/20ebf4b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/69bf4ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5c852c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Illinois House Speaker Emanuel "Chris" Welch talks to reporters late Wednesday in Springfield. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5c852c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/389f7fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e5c852c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F78%2Ff3%2Fdee59edd43018c0f59473ea4206b%2Fimg-9256.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Illinois House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch talks to reporters late Wednesday in Springfield. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Matt Trunfio/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Welch said his focus Wednesday was on a constitutional amendment intended to protect voting rights and a megaprojects bill that incentivizes entities like the Bears to stay in Illinois.</p><p>Benton, who did not respond to a request for comment Thursday, voted in favor of both of those proposals. </p><p></p><p>Benton, a two-term lawmaker, is up for re-election in the 97th District this year. He breezed through a Democratic primary unopposed and now faces Republican nominee Gabby Shanahan.</p><p>When asked to comment, Shanahan did not directly address Benton, saying only, "We deserve answers, not silence.”</p><p>"Right now, the people of our district are being let down," Shanahan said in a written statement. "I’m offering voters a better alternative: Someone who will show up and work with integrity for them."</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p></p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Rich Eisen's evasive commentary on Dianna Russini-Mike Vrabel saga indicative of NFL media's coverage
entries.summary: <p>Rich Eisen is not a man of few words. As the host of a daily three-hour talk show, he can’t be. But on Thursday, he chose brevity, which his listeners could construe as a cop-out.</p><p>As the Dianna Russini-Mike Vrabel saga began another chapter with the news that the Patriots coach won’t be with his team beginning Saturday, the third day of the NFL Draft, Eisen addressed the matter early in his ESPN show, evasively hemming and hawing for three minutes:</p><p>“I’ll be very honest with you, folks. I have avoided talking about this subject matter because it involves two consenting, married adults and what they were doing in their spare time with each other, allegedly … . I understand that there’s professionalism at stake for both individuals.</p><p>“But now the fact that Mike Vrabel’s not gonna be in the draft room … on Saturday because he is taking a moment in time to seek counseling, apparently, with his family … I’ve never heard of anything like this. … Whatever both he and Dianna need to do … I tip my cap to both people that I’ve known for quite some time. … I was kind of shocked to see that headline.</p><p>“Just wanted to address that to start our program here today from the draft city of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Kurt Warner’s about to join us …”</p><p>Uh, Kurt Warner can wait, Rich. How can you not delve into something that “shocked” you?</p><p>It was another example of NFL media types treating a consequential story not with kid gloves, but by not touching it at all.</p><p>Initially, Russini and Vrabel flatly dismissed the suggestive photos of them that arose in the New York Post earlier this month. Since then, Russini resigned as The Athletic’s lead NFL reporter, and Vrabel is set to take a hiatus. The Post revealed more damning photos Thursday.</p><p>But because both are well-liked in NFL circles, they’re being protected by their media colleagues. Russini and Vrabel shouldn’t be given cover; they should be covered. Eisen was right to say “professionalism” is at stake. That includes his. He lacked it by avoiding the subject.</p><p>Others have, too. Greg Bedard of Boston Sports Journal said on The Sports Hub in Boston that it wasn’t his job to cover extramarital affairs, that the only reason the station was talking about it was because the draft had “no juice.”</p><p>ESPN’s website didn’t include Vrabel’s upcoming absence among it’s “Top Headlines” on Thursday afternoon. The NFL Coach of the Year missing eight picks during the draft should qualify as a top headline all day.</p><p>Ethics involving relationships and sourcing are industrywide issues that should be discussed, whether or not we know the offenders.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest
